cross_connections.display_label: Recruited the Connecticut minister who founded Austinburg's church

narrative_blocks.header: Rode 600 miles alone to recruit a minister
narrative_blocks.body: In spring 1810 Sybil concluded that the new settlement of Austinburg needed a settled Congregational minister. She rode alone by horseback over six hundred miles to Bristol Connecticut — clothing in a portmanteau, thirty-day journey through unsettled forest — to persuade Sally and Rev. Giles Hooker Cowles to migrate to the Western Reserve. Her ride succeeded; the Cowles family arrived in Austinburg in 1811.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughters all attended Litchfield Female Academy
narrative_blocks.body: Four of her daughters attended the Litchfield Female Academy — Sybil Webb in 1818, Chloe, Florilla, and Sophia. The Academy founded by Sarah Pierce in 1792 was among the earliest formal secondary schools for women in the United States.

sources.short: Williams Brothers 'History of Ashtabula County' (1878), biography of Rev. Giles Hooker Cowles
Edward Hooker 1909, entry #688 (re: marriage of Rev. Eliphalet Austin Jr to Sally Berrien Cowles, identifies mother as Sibyl Dudley)

career.title: Connecticut Land Company partner
career.notes: Joined the Connecticut Land Company, formed to settle and develop the section of western Connecticut later called the Connecticut Western Reserve in Ohio. Purchased wide tracts including present-day Austinburg Township, which bears his name.

career.title: Founder
career.notes: Settled at present-day Austinburg in 1799. The township is named for him. Responsible for carrying the mail in the region; operated a cattle-dealing operation.

landmarks.notes: Built the Eliphalet Austin House Austinburg OH 1815 (per Wikipedia; 1811 per local tradition). NRHP listed 1975. Heavily modified Italianate style by his grandson Louis B. Austin in the 1860s.

cross_connections.display_label: Co-founders of Austinburg church and Western Reserve College

narrative_blocks.header: Founder of Austinburg Township, Ohio Western Reserve
narrative_blocks.body: A Connecticut Land Company partner, Eliphalet purchased wide tracts across the Connecticut Western Reserve in the late 1790s and settled at present-day Austinburg in 1799 — the township still bears his name. He carried the mail across the region, operated a cattle-dealing business, and served in the Ohio Senate 1829-1831.

narrative_blocks.header: His wife rode horseback to recruit a minister
narrative_blocks.body: In spring 1810, his wife Sybil Dudley Austin rode six hundred miles by horseback to Bristol Connecticut — alone, with her wardrobe in a portmanteau — to recruit Rev. Giles Hooker Cowles to settle Ashtabula County's first Congregational minister. Giles, his wife Sally, and their eight children arrived in Austinburg the following summer 1811. The Austin-Cowles partnership defined Austinburg's pioneer era.

narrative_blocks.header: His Austinburg house is on the National Register
narrative_blocks.body: Eliphalet built the brick house at 1879 State Route 45 Austinburg in 1811 (or 1815 per Wikipedia). His grandson Louis B. Austin remodelled it in the Italianate style during the 1860s.

sources.short: Williams Brothers 'History of Ashtabula County' (1878), biography of Rev. Giles Hooker Cowles (re: Mrs. Austin's recruitment ride)
National Register of Historic Places, NRHP reference No. 75001313
Cowles Families in America #688 — Sally Berrien Cowles Austin
Find a Grave Memorial 47267856 (Rev. Eliphalet Austin)
Edward Hooker 1909, entry #688 (re: Sally Berrien Cowles + husband)

institutions.notes: Member of the Grand River Presbytery board that founded Western Reserve College at Hudson; charter 1826.

narrative_blocks.header: Recording secretary of Ashtabula's antislavery society
narrative_blocks.body: Eliphalet served as recording secretary at the May 1834 founding of the Anti-Slavery Society of Ashtabula County. The society's preamble declared all men created free and equal and called for 'the utter extinction of slavery by immediate emancipation.' His own father-in-law's parsonage and his household served as an Underground Railroad station for fleeing slaves.

sources.short: Cowles Families in America #688 — Sally Berrien Cowles Austin

narrative_blocks.header: First Cowles bride of the Austinburg generation
narrative_blocks.body: At twenty-three Sally married a son of Judge Eliphalet Austin in 1819, joining the founding family of the Ohio settlement her father had been recruited to plant. The wedding fused two of Austinburg's pioneer households.

narrative_blocks.header: Their farmhouse hid passengers on the Underground Railroad
narrative_blocks.body: The Austin household ran one of Ashtabula County's documented Underground Railroad stations through the 1830s and 1840s, sheltering fugitives moving north toward Lake Erie. Sally kept the kitchen fires going for travelers who never appeared in the daylight record.

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed at forty-one — outlived him by thirty-five years
narrative_blocks.body: Her husband died in 1837 and left her a young widow with the household to keep. She managed the farm and raised the children alone for thirty-five years until her own death in 1872.

burial.plot_notes: Austinburg Center Cemetery, Austinburg, Ashtabula County, Ohio.

burialCemetery.name: Austinburg Center Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Cemetery of the Congregational church founded by Rev. Giles Hooker Cowles. Contains graves of Rev. Cowles, his wife Sallie White Cowles, and son Edward Giles Hooker Cowles.
